title: Address to the Opening of the General Debate of the 79th Session of the General Assembly
occasion: 79th Session of the United Nations General Assembly

paragraphs.text: Mr. President, Excellencies, ladies and gentlemen. We gather at a time when our world is in a state of profound upheaval. Conflicts are raging. Inequalities are deepening. The climate crisis is accelerating. Trust in institutions is eroding. And new technologies are advancing faster than our capacity to govern them. We are, I fear, at an inflection point.

I have been Secretary-General for nearly eight years. In that time, I have seen the multilateral system tested as never before. But I have also seen its indispensable value. No single nation, no matter how powerful, can address the challenges we face alone. Climate change, pandemics, nuclear proliferation, artificial intelligence — these are inherently global problems that demand global solutions. The alternative to multilateralism is not freedom. It is chaos.

Let me begin with the devastating situation in the Middle East. The conflict in Gaza has produced catastrophic humanitarian consequences. Tens of thousands of civilians have been killed. The vast majority of the population has been displaced, many of them multiple times. Essential infrastructure — hospitals, schools, water systems — has been destroyed. International humanitarian law must be respected by all parties. It is not optional. It is not conditional. It applies always and everywhere.

I reiterate my call for an immediate ceasefire in Gaza, the unconditional release of all hostages, and unimpeded humanitarian access to all those in need. The cycle of violence, occupation, and suffering must end. The only path to lasting peace is a two-state solution, with Israel and Palestine living side by side in peace and security, with Jerusalem as the capital of both states. This is not merely a policy preference. It is a legal and moral imperative.

In Sudan, a brutal war has displaced millions and created what may become the world’s worst humanitarian crisis. In Ukraine, the war continues to exact a terrible toll on civilians while threatening the foundations of the international order. In Myanmar, the Sahel, Haiti, and elsewhere, conflicts and instability are destroying lives and hopes. The Security Council must rise to its responsibilities. When it fails to act, the consequences are measured in human suffering.

The climate crisis is not a future threat. It is a present reality. This year is on track to be the hottest year in recorded history. We have witnessed catastrophic floods, devastating wildfires, and extreme heat events on every continent. The science is unequivocal: we are not on track to meet the goals of the Paris Agreement. Current national climate pledges, even if fully implemented, would lead to a temperature increase of approximately 2.5 degrees Celsius. This would be catastrophic.

I call on all governments to submit new nationally determined contributions that are consistent with the 1.5-degree limit. This means tripling renewable energy capacity and doubling energy efficiency by 2030. It means phasing out fossil fuels in a just and equitable manner. And it means delivering on the financial commitments made to developing countries. The loss and damage fund agreed at COP28 must be capitalized at a level commensurate with the scale of the crisis.

The small island developing states, which contribute virtually nothing to global emissions, face an existential threat from rising seas. For them, the climate crisis is not an environmental issue. It is a question of survival. We owe them not just our sympathy but concrete action and adequate resources. Climate justice is not a luxury. It is a necessity.

Artificial intelligence represents perhaps the most transformative technology in human history. It holds immense promise for accelerating scientific discovery, improving healthcare, and advancing sustainable development. But it also carries profound risks. The potential for AI to be used in autonomous weapons systems, for mass surveillance, for the manipulation of information, and for the entrenchment of bias and discrimination is deeply concerning.

We cannot afford to repeat the mistakes we made with social media, where we allowed a transformative technology to spread globally without adequate governance and are now grappling with its corrosive effects on democracy and mental health. The governance of artificial intelligence must be inclusive, must respect human rights, and must ensure that the benefits are shared broadly. The Global Digital Compact adopted at the Summit of the Future is an important step, but much more is needed.

Global inequality remains a fundamental threat to peace and stability. The gap between the richest and the poorest continues to widen. Developing countries are spending more on debt service than on education and health combined. The international financial architecture is failing the countries that need it most. When a nation struck by a climate disaster is forced to borrow at punitive rates to rebuild, something is fundamentally broken in the system.

I have called for reforms to the international financial system, including a new Bretton Woods moment. We need to reform the governance structures of the international financial institutions to give developing countries a greater voice. We need to expand access to affordable finance for sustainable development. And we need to address the crushing debt burden that prevents many countries from investing in their own people and their own futures.

The nuclear threat has returned to levels not seen since the Cold War. Arms control agreements have been abandoned or allowed to lapse. Nuclear arsenals are being modernized and, in some cases, expanded. The risk of nuclear escalation — whether by design, miscalculation, or accident — is higher than at any point in decades. I urge all nuclear-armed states to recommit to the goal of a world free of nuclear weapons and to take immediate steps to reduce nuclear risks.

The Sustainable Development Goals, adopted in 2015, represented a shared vision for a better world by 2030. We are now past the halfway point, and progress is deeply off track. Only 17 percent of the targets are on track to be met. On some goals, progress has actually reversed. The pandemic, conflicts, and the climate crisis have all taken their toll. But so has insufficient political will and inadequate investment. The SDGs are not a wish list. They are a blueprint for survival.

The Summit of the Future, which you have just concluded, produced important commitments: the Pact for the Future, the Global Digital Compact, and the Declaration on Future Generations. These are meaningful steps forward. But commitments on paper must become actions on the ground. The credibility of this institution depends on our collective capacity to deliver on the promises we make.

Gender equality remains one of the most powerful levers for progress, and one of the most neglected. At the current rate, gender parity will not be achieved for another three centuries. This is not only unjust. It is irrational. Societies that exclude half their populations from full participation pay an enormous price in lost potential, in economic underperformance, and in diminished resilience.

Young people are the most underrepresented constituency in global decision-making, and yet they will bear the greatest consequences of the decisions we make today. I urge governments to lower barriers to youth participation in political life, to invest in education and employment for young people, and to take their voices seriously. The future belongs to them, and they have every right to shape it.

The challenges before us are daunting. But despair is not a strategy, and cynicism is not a policy. The United Nations, for all its imperfections, remains the only institution with the universality, the legitimacy, and the mandate to convene the world around common solutions. I urge all member states to recommit to the principles of the Charter: peace, justice, human rights, and the equal dignity of every human being. The stakes could not be higher. Thank you.
